content.text: 1. Alkali Metals (Group 1)
Elements: Li (Lithium), Na (Sodium), K (Potassium), Rb (Rubidium), Cs (Caesium), Fr (Francium). Sab s-block elements hun — outermost shell ma 1 valence electron.
1.1 Physical Properties

① Soft metals — knife le cut garna sakkincha.
② Low density — Li, Na, K float on water.
③ Shiny, silvery appearance.
④ Low melting and boiling points — group tala jane sanga decrease huncha.
1.2 Chemical Properties

① Highly reactive — especially with water, oxygen, and halogens.
② Form strong bases (alkalis) when dissolved in water.
③ Group tala jane sanga reactivity badhcha.

content.name: Reaction with Water
content.note: Vigorous reaction; NaOH is a strong base. Li is slowest, Cs is most explosive among stable alkali metals.

content.name: Reaction with Oxygen
content.formula: 4Li + O_2 \rightarrow 2Li_2O \quad (\text{oxide}) \\ 2Na + O_2 \rightarrow Na_2O_2 \quad (\text{peroxide}) \\ K + O_2 \rightarrow KO_2 \quad (\text{superoxide})
content.note: Li → oxide; Na → peroxide; K, Rb, Cs → superoxide. Product type changes down the group.

content.name: Reaction with Halogens
content.note: Form ionic halides. All alkali metals react vigorously with halogens.

content.text: 1.3 Trends Down Group 1

• Reactivity ↑ (ionisation energy decreases → easier to lose electron)
• Density ↑ (except Li < Na > K anomaly is absent; general trend is ↑)
• Melting & boiling point ↓ (weaker metallic bonding down the group)
1.4 Uses of Alkali Metals

• Na → table salt (NaCl), soap making, organic synthesis
• K → fertilizers (KCl, KNO₃)
• Li → rechargeable batteries, psychiatric medicine (Li₂CO₃)
2. Alkaline Earth Metals (Group 2)
Elements: Be (Beryllium), Mg (Magnesium), Ca (Calcium), Sr (Strontium), Ba (Barium), Ra (Radium). Outermost shell ma 2 valence electrons — s-block elements.
2.1 Physical Properties

① Harder than alkali metals (2 valence electrons → stronger metallic bond).
② Higher melting and boiling points than Group 1.
③ Less dense than alkali metals (generally).
④ Silvery-white appearance.
2.2 Chemical Properties

① Reactivity lower than alkali metals but still reactive.
② Be ra Mg react slowly with water; Ca, Sr, Ba react readily.
③ React with oxygen to form oxides.
④ React with halogens to form halides.

content.name: Reaction with Water
content.note: Be does NOT react with water; Mg reacts only with hot water/steam; Ca, Sr, Ba react with cold water. Reactivity ↑ down the group.

content.name: Reaction with Oxygen
content.note: Form basic oxides. MgO and CaO are widely used in industry.

content.name: Reaction with Halogens
content.note: Form ionic dihalides (MX₂). CaCl₂ is used as a desiccant.

content.text: 2.3 Trends Down Group 2

• Reactivity ↑ (ionisation energy decreases down the group)
• Density ↑
• Melting & boiling point ↓ (weaker metallic bonding)
2.4 Uses of Alkaline Earth Metals

• Ca → cement, steel manufacturing, plaster of Paris
• Mg → lightweight alloys (aircraft, cars), fireworks (bright white flame)
• Ba → drilling fluids (BaSO₄), paints
3. Comparison: Alkali vs Alkaline Earth Metals

Property | Alkali Metals (Group 1) | Alkaline Earth Metals (Group 2)
Valence electrons | 1 | 2
Reactivity | Very high | High, but less than Group 1
Density | Low | Higher than Group 1
Hardness | Soft | Harder than Group 1
Reaction with water | Vigorous | Less vigorous (Be/Mg: slow)
Melting point | Low | Higher than Group 1

content.label: 🎓 Key Points to Remember:
content.text:
✓ Both groups are s-block elements.
✓ Alkali metals → 1 valence e⁻; Alkaline earth metals → 2 valence e⁻.
✓ Alkali metals: extremely reactive, form strong bases (MOH).
✓ Alkaline earth metals: reactive but less so; form less soluble hydroxides.
✓ Reactivity ↑ down both groups (ionisation energy ↓).
✓ Melting/boiling point ↓ down both groups.
✓ Na forms peroxide (Na₂O₂); Li forms normal oxide (Li₂O); K forms superoxide (KO₂) — classic MCQ!
✓ Be is anomalous — does not react with water and shows amphoteric behaviour.
